Name: curvature_aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing via Hessian Diagnostics
Short Hypothesis: The local curvature of the activation space, quantified by Hessian eigenvalues, provides a robust diagnostic for precision allocation in mixed-precision inference, enabling dynamic adaptation to input distributions without training or calibration.
Related Work: Existing methods for mixed-precision inference, such as Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR), rely on activation covariance geometry to allocate precision. While effective, these approaches do not explicitly consider second-order curvature information, which can provide deeper insights into quantization sensitivity. Our proposal distinguishes itself by leveraging Hessian-based curvature diagnostics, offering a novel pathway for precision allocation grounded in second-order optimization theory.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference offers significant computational savings but often relies on static profiling or heuristic calibration, limiting adaptability to dynamic input distributions. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates precision based on the local curvature of the activation space. By estimating Hessian eigenvalues on-the-fly using randomized numerical linear algebra, CAPR identifies tokens residing in high-curvature regions that require higher precision for stable computation. We theoretically ground this mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds linking Hessian conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized curvature diagnostics, CAPR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Hessian Estimation Efficiency - Validate the runtime efficiency of randomized Hessian eigenvalue estimation on single-GPU setups using synthetic datasets. Metrics: Wall-clock time, memory overhead.
Experiment 2: Precision Allocation - Evaluate the precision allocation policy on WILDS benchmarks, comparing CAPR to static mixed-precision baselines. Metrics: Accuracy, throughput, latency.
Experiment 3: Generalization Stability - Measure the generalization stability of CAPR under distribution shifts using PAC-Bayes bounds. Metrics: Risk bounds, empirical risk.
Experiment 4: Ablation Study - Ablate the impact of Hessian-based diagnostics by comparing CAPR to a covariance-based variant (similar to SQR). Metrics: Accuracy, throughput, latency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Hessian estimation may introduce computational overhead, limiting the feasibility of real-time inference.
Risk 2: The theoretical bounds may not tightly capture the empirical generalization behavior, reducing the practical utility of the diagnostics.
Limitation 1: The approach assumes access to second-order information, which may not be feasible in all architectures.
Limitation 2: The framework is currently limited to single-GPU setups, requiring further optimization for distributed environments.
